Rationale: Consensus high; private key control and cryptographic ownership strongly emphasized in whitepaper and 2011 forum discussions; minor variance from enforcement evidence.
Exchange rates form freely on Mt. Gox and early markets via bid/ask; no central price setting observed in 2011 data (Mt. Gox 2011 activity; bitcointalk.org trading threads).
Mining rewards and transaction fees drive participation; GPU mining profitability and speculation dominate 2011 forum discussions (bitcointalk.org mining threads 2011).
No forced redistribution; voluntary mining rewards and transfers dominant in 2011 evidence (Bitcoin whitepaper; bitcointalk.org 2011 consensus).
No entitlements; voluntary community donations and charity drives exist but are not protocol-enforced (bitcointalk.org charity threads 2011).
Blockchain maintains continuous verifiable ownership chain from genesis; no resets or major forks by end-2011 (block explorer data 2011).
Protocol largely single-implementation; early altcoin experiments like Namecoin appear but are external (bitcointalk.org altcoin threads 2011).
Network survives 2011 price crashes, hacks, and technical issues; uptime and node growth indicate medium-term viability (bitcointalk.org 2011 status threads).
GPU mining centralization and exchange vulnerabilities allow some extraction; protocol limits systemic parasitism (bitcointalk.org mining centralization debates 2011).
Proof-of-work difficulty adjustment and GPU mining race create vigorous bounded rivalry (Bitcoin whitepaper; bitcointalk.org GPU mining threads 2011).
Mt. Gox hack (June 2011) and price volatility serve as meaningful existential filters; network continues (CoinDesk 2011 coverage; bitcointalk.org hack threads).
Early merchant adoption, Silk Road usage, and payment experimentation expand digital cash niche (2011 news coverage; bitcointalk.org merchant threads).
Consensus rules and longest-chain acceptance maintain strong network identity and validity boundaries (Bitcoin whitepaper; bitcointalk.org consensus discussions 2011).
Overflow bug fix (2010) and rapid client patches in 2011 show reasonable repair capacity; shim-adjusted due to limited automated detection (bitcointalk.org bug fix threads 2011).
Nodes reject invalid blocks/transactions; exchange-level fraud remains hard to detect automatically (Mt. Gox 2011 incidents; bitcointalk.org security threads).
Protocol shows some modularity in clients/mining; early but not fractal in 2011 (bitcointalk.org 2011).
Distributed blockchain storage durable across nodes in 2011 (Bitcoin whitepaper; bitcointalk.org 2011).
